paper_title: Compress-Distill: Reasoning Trace Compression for Efficient Knowledge Distillation

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Reasoning models produce long chain-of-thought traces that are costly to distill and encourage verbose student outputs. We study post-hoc compression of such traces before knowledge distillation. Two teachers, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B and gpt-oss-120B, generate about 283k correct traces each; two instruction-tuned models then compress them to 8.6-21.0% of their original character length. Across a 48-run main grid plus seven Qwen-teacher truncation ablations, compressed traces reduce training tokens to 12-30% of raw, speed up training by 2.0-7.6x, and shorten inference outputs by 3-19x with smaller reductions under the shorter gpt-oss teacher. However, raw traces retain the highest downstream accuracy at every scale and for both teachers. A length-matched raw-trace truncation ablation shows that compression is not merely benefiting from a smaller token budget: model-compressed traces usually beat or match naive truncation, especially for smaller students, while maintaining shorter inference outputs. Overall, reasoning-trace compression offers an accuracy-efficiency trade-off rather than a free improvement: students retain up to 96% of raw-trace accuracy while gaining up to 18x higher per-token efficiency, and at the 0.8B scale under LoRA compressed traces narrow the raw-vs-compressed gap but do not exceed raw.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: Compress-Distill: Reasoning Trace Compression for Efficient Knowledge Distillation Maxime Griot1,, Paul Steven Scotti2, Tanishq Mathew Abraham2 1Université catholique de Louvain, 2Sophont Inc. 6 2 0 2 4 ] . [ 1 8 8 9 5 0 . 6 0 6 2 : r a

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Reasoning models produce long chain-ofthought traces that are costly to distill and encourage verbose student outputs. We study post-hoc compression of such traces before knowledge distillation. Two teachers, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B and gpt-oss-120B, generate about 283k correct traces each; two instruction-tuned models then compress them to 8.621.0% of their original character length. Across 48-run main grid plus seven Qwen-teacher truncation ablations, compressed traces reduce training tokens to 1230% of raw, speed up training by 2.0 7.6, and shorten inference outputs by 319 with smaller reductions under the shorter gptoss teacher. However, raw traces retain the highest downstream accuracy at every scale and for both teachers. length-matched rawtrace truncation ablation shows that compression is not merely benefiting from smaller token budget: model-compressed traces usually beat or match naive truncation, especially for smaller students, while maintaining shorter inference outputs. Overall, reasoning-trace compression offers an accuracyefficiency tradeoff rather than free improvement: students retain up to 96% of raw-trace accuracy while gaining up to 18 higher per-token efficiency, and at the 0.8B scale under LoRA compressed traces narrow the raw-vs-compressed gap but do not exceed raw.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Reasoning-tuned models such as Qwen3 (Yang et al., 2025a) and DeepSeek-R1 (Guo et al., 2025) often produce long chain-of-thought (CoT) traces. These traces can improve step-by-step reasoning (Wei et al., 2022; Kojima et al., 2022), but they also increase supervised fine-tuning cost and teach students to generate verbose, expensive outputs. This work was performed while serving as an independent consultant for Sophont Inc. 1 We ask whether teacher reasoning can be compressed before distillation, and what accuracy efficiency trade-off this creates. Our pipeline generates correct traces with large teacher, rewrites them with instruction-tuned compressor models, and fine-tunes students on raw, compressed, or answer-only targets. Across two teachers, four students, two training regimes, 48-run main grid, and seven additional Qwen-teacher truncation ablations, we evaluate downstream accuracy, training cost, inference length, truncation, and pertoken efficiency. We find that compression sharply reduces cost but underperforms raw traces at every evaluated scale and under both teachers. Still, compression provides worthwhile efficiency trade-off: compressed traces cut training tokens to 1230% of raw, reduce wall-clock training by 2.07.6, and shorten inference reasoning by 319. The answer-only baseline is cheapest but performs worst and is unstable under full fine-tuning. Our contribution is an empirical characterization of reasoning-trace compression as practical trade-off rather than free improvement. We provide: 1. pipeline for trace generation, compression, and student fine-tuning. 2. compression analysis across teachers, domains, and compressor models. 3. 48-run study reporting training cost, downstream accuracy, inference length, truncation, and per-token efficiency. 4. length-matched truncation ablation testing whether compressed traces help beyond reduced training-token budget. 5. An analysis of including small-student termination failures and unstable answer-only training. failure modes,

sections.title: 3 Method
sections.content: Chain-of-thought reasoning. Wei et al. (2022) showed that prompting LLMs to produce stepby-step reasoning improves arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning. Kojima et al. (2022) showed zero-shot CoT prompting elicits reasoning without task-specific examples. Reasoning-tuned models such as DeepSeek-R1 (Guo et al., 2025) and QwQ (Team, 2025) are explicitly trained to emit long deliberation traces. Knowledge distillation for reasoning. Ho et al. (2023), Magister et al. (2023), and Shridhar et al. (2023) all show that fine-tuning smaller models on traces produced by larger teacher transfers reasoning ability, typically using the teachers full verbose trace as the target. We instead study step in between: rewriting the teachers trace with separate compressor before distillation. Reasoning trace optimisation. Several works seek to reduce the cost of long reasoning traces. Xia et al. (2025) prune low-importance CoT tokens and fine-tune models to learn controllable shortcuts, while Kang et al. (2025) use stronger model to generate shorter CoTs for training. Other approaches control reasoning length through token-budget estimation, RL, pruning, or self-training (Han et al., 2025; Aggarwal and Welleck, 2025; Luo et al., 2025; Munkhbat et al., 2025), and Zhang et al. (2026) explicitly studies performance-preserving reasoning compression. Our work is closest in spirit to this line, but differs in evaluating post-hoc model-based rewriting of verified teacher traces as distillation data intervention across two teachers, four students, and both LoRA and full fine-tuning. Inference-time efficiency for reasoning. complementary line targets the decoding budget directly, with overthinking analyses, adaptive slow/fast reasoning, and sketch-like rationales (Chen et al., 2025; Shen et al., 2025; Aytes et al., 2025; Xu et al., 2025; Yang et al., 2025b). We keep greedy decoding and an 8,192-token cap fixed to isolate the training-time intervention. The regimes are complementary: training-time compression pays one-time data cost for shorter default outputs, while decoding-time controls are reusable across checkpoints but always-on at inference. 2 COMPRESS-DISTILL is three-stage pipeline; each stage is independently resumable and writes JSONL outputs that feed the next stage.

sections.title: 3.1 Stage 1: Trace Generation
sections.content: Given teacher MT and benchmark questions {qi}N i=1, we sample response ri = MT (qi) that contains reasoning trace ti (within <think>. . . </think>) and final answer ai. We extract ai using type-specific cascade and verify against the canonical answer . If ai = , we resample up to times. Only correct traces are retained. Answers are extracted with type-specific cascade (boxed{}, ####, the answer is, or fallbacks for numbers/letters/booleans/LATEX) and matched against . All pending questions are submitted in single batch to vLLM (Kwon et al., 2023), and questions answered correctly are removed before the next rejection-sampling round.

sections.title: 3.2 Stage 2: Trace Compression
sections.content: For each correct triple (qi, ti, ai) from Stage 1, we apply compressor MC with single generic prompt that asks for shorter version of the reasoning preserving the essential logical steps, key insights, and the final answer. The compressed trace ˆti = MC(qi, ti, ai) is generated with low temperature (τ =0.3). We measure the perexample character-level compression ratio ρi = ˆti/ti. We run two compressors independently, producing two parallel compressed datasets that differ in compression aggressiveness and style.

sections.title: 3.3 Stage 3: Student Training
sections.content: We fine-tune student models MS on raw traces, compressed traces, or an answer-only ablation that removes the entire <think>. . . </think> block, using chat template: User: qi Asst: think ti /think ai, where ti {ti, ˆti, } (the last replaces the think block with the empty string, leaving only the final answer). Training uses next-token prediction on assistant tokens only, with sample packing. We evaluate grid over two teachers MT (Qwen3.5-397B-A17B and gpt-oss-120B), four (Qwen3.5-0.8B-Base, Qwen3.5-9Bstudents Base, Llama-3.1-8B, and gpt-oss-20B), four Llama-70B-compressed, data sources (raw, and answer-only), Ministral-14B-compressed, and two one-epoch methods (LoRA and full / FSDP fine-tuning) (Hu et al., 2022). Answer-only is run only once because it uses no teacher trace and is omitted for the gpt-oss-20B student for the same reason. This gives 48 main-grid runs: 27 under the Qwen teacher and 21 under the gpt-oss teacher. To test whether compressed traces help because they are better supervision rather than simply shorter supervision, we add tracestruncated ablation for the Qwen-teacher runs. For each example, we tokenize the raw Qwen trace with the Ministral tokenizer and truncate it to the token length of the corresponding Ministralcompressed trace, allowing 1 token mismatch after decode/re-encode. The final answer is kept unchanged. This produces computeequivalent raw-trace baseline at the same perexample training-token budget as the most aggressive compressor. We train the same seven Qwen-teacher student/method configurations as the reasoning-trace grid.

sections.title: 4 Experimental Setup
sections.content: Trace generation and downstream evaluation use the in-distribution datasets in Table 1: ten datasets across four domains plus two MMLU subject groups (Hendrycks et al., 2021). SVAMP (Patel et al., 2021) and GPQA Diamond (Rein et al., 2024) lack labeled held-out splits and are used for trace generation only. Downstream evaluation also includes OOD-reasoning datasets (HellaSwag (Zellers et al., 2019), WinoGrande (Sakaguchi et al., 2021), PIQA (Bisk et al., 2020), ARC-Easy (Clark et al., 2018), BoolQ (Clark et al., 2019), TruthfulQA (Lin et al., 2022)) and OOD-knowledge groups (MMLU humanities, social sciences, other). All training uses Axolotl with BF16, FlashAttention 2 (Dao, 2024), CutCrossEntropy (Wijmans et al., 2025), 16,384-token sequences with sample packing, and 5% validation split. LoRA runs use rank 64, α=32, dropout 0.05, all attention and MLP projections, LR 1104 with cosine schedule and 10% warmup, one epoch, and 8-bit AdamW. Full fine-tuning uses LR 2 105 for one epoch; the 8B, 9B, and 20B students use FSDP v2 with full sharding and activation checkpointing, while the 0.8B model fits on single GPU.

sections.title: Each student
sections.content: is evaluated with the same template used during training. Generachat tion is greedy with an 8,192-token cap. We report per-group accuracy for Math, Science, Medicine, Common-sense, OOD-Reason., and OOD-Knownledge. Overall accuracy across all records, inference output length, and per-token efficiency metric are defined in Section 5.4.

sections.title: 5.1 Trace Generation
sections.content: The Qwen3.5-397B-A17B teacher yields 283,335 verified-correct traces across the trace-generation Accuracy and verbosity vary widely: pool. on easy arithmetic (MultiArith, 99.0%) the teacher emits short traces (mean 1,750 characters), while on graduate-level science (GPQA Diamond, 68.2%) it averages 18,734-character tracesmore than 10 longer. This per-problem variance is what motivates compression. The gpt-oss-120B teacher (high reasoning) answers 281,911 questions correctly with traces that are roughly half as long character-for-character (e.g., MultiArith mean 716, GPQA Diamond mean 13,407): similar accuracy as Qwen3.5397B-A17B, substantially less verbosity. The two teachers thus probe opposite ends of the verbosity axis, which determines how much room downstream compression has to work. Per-dataset statistics for both teachers are in Appendix A, Table 4.

sections.title: 5.2 Compression
sections.content: Table 2 and Figure 1 summarize compression for each (teacher, compressor) pair; per-dataset statistics are reported in Appendix B, Table 5. Under the Qwen teacher, Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct (Dubey et al., 2024) compresses traces to mean ratio of ρ=0.142, while Ministral-3-14B-Instruct2512 (Liu et al., 2026) is more aggressive at ρ=0.086. Under the gpt-oss teacher (OpenAI et al., 2025), compression is milder, with mean ratios of ρ=0.210 for Llama-70B and ρ=0.147 for Ministral-14B. Thus, Ministral-14B is consistently the more aggressive compressor, while gpt-oss traces retain higher compression ratios because the raw teacher traces are already substantially shorter. Compression is strongly dataset-dependent. GPQA Diamond compresses hardest, especially under the Qwen teacher (ρ=0.089 for Llama70B and ρ=0.054 for Ministral-14B), whereas

sections.title: Length
sections.content: ρ Qwen3.5-397B Llama-3.3-70B Qwen3.5-397B Ministral-3-14B Llama-3.3-70B gpt-oss-120B Ministral-3-14B gpt-oss-120B 1,008 613 793 554 0.142 0.086 0.210 0.147 Table 2: Aggregate compression statistics. Length is mean compressed length; ρ is mean compressed / original length. shorter/easier datasets such as MultiArith and ARC leave less room for compression. This pattern suggests that longer reasoning traces contain more removable deliberation. Figure 2 shows that compression removes much of the long right tail: after compression, all but the longest 10% of traces are shorter than 500 estimated tokens under both compressors, although rare long outliers remain. Under the gpt-oss teacher, the qualitative picture is broadly similar: GPQA Diamond compresses hardest, Ministral-14B is more aggressive than Llama-70B across datasets, and the shortest/easiest datasets leave the least room for compression. However, the absolute amount removed is smaller because there is less redundancy to remove from teacher whose raw traces are already 2 shorter. Consequently, the compressors do not bring the two teachers to common target length: gpt-oss compressed traces remain shorter in absolute terms, but their compression ratios are higher than under the more verbose Qwen teacher.

sections.title: 5.3 Training Loss and Efficiency
sections.content: Figure 3 shows the loss curves; full wall-clock and token counts are reported in Appendix C, Table 6. Within the three reasoning-trace sources, fitraining loss under the Qwen teacher ornal ders the data sources consistently in every (student, method) configuration: raw < Llama-70B < Ministral-14B (e.g., Qwen3.5-9B: 0.314 / 0.422 / 0.478). This matches the downstream-accuracy ordering for that teacher (Section 5.4), so loss is faithful ranker for these comparisons. The answeronly baseline is an outlier: its loss is sometimes lower than raw (0.289 for Qwen3.5-9B), but its downstream accuracy is far worse. The truncated ablation is another warning that training loss alone is not supervision quality. Under the Qwen teacher, truncated targets finish with lower loss than the corresponding Ministral targets in every configuration, and in some full-finetuning runs even lower than raw. Yet downstream accuracy is usually below the compressed runs, especially for Llama-3.1-8B and Qwen3.5-0.8B. Matching the token budget by cutting off the raw trace therefore makes the next-token problem easier without reliably preserving the answer-bearing reasoning. Under the gpt-oss teacher this ordering inverts: compressed sources reach lower final loss than raw for all larger-student configurations, although the 0.8B Ministral runs remain slightly above raw. The downstream ordering, however, still has raw > compressed (Section 5.4), so under this teacher loss is no longer faithful ranker even within the three reasoning-trace sources. The wall-clock benefit of compression tracks but lags the token reduction, and is smaller under the shorter-trace teacher. Under the Qwen teacher, the 0.8B student speeds up 5.87.6 at 1216% of raw tokens; the 8B/9B students gain 3.24.9; gpt-oss-20B sits in between (5.16.3). Under the Figure 1: Mean compression ratio by domain (lower is more aggressive), one panel per teacher. The gpt-oss teachers compressed traces compress to higher ratio (less compression) than the Qwen teachers at every (compressor, domain) combination because the source traces are already substantially shorter.

sections.title: 5.4 Downstream Evaluation
sections.content: Table 3 reports overall accuracy for the main-grid reasoning-trace runs and Figure 4 plots accuracy against median reasoning tokens, including the Qwen-teacher truncation ablation. Full per-group accuracy, including answer-only rows, is in Appendix  (Table 7)  ; matching inference statistics are in Appendix  (Table 9)  . Accuracy: raw wins at every scale, under both teachers. Raw teacher traces yield the highest overall accuracy in every (teacher, student, method) configuration we evaluate, including the Qwen-teacher truncation ablation (Tables 3 and 8). Under full fine-tuning, representative raw / Llama70B / Ministral-14B rows are 0.866 / 0.834 / 0.817 for Qwen3.5-9B under the Qwen teacher and 0.844 / 0.776 / 0.767 for gpt-oss-20B under the gpt-oss teacher. The gap is largest at 8B/20B and smallest at 0.8B LoRA, where the two compressors are within 1 SE of each other. The qualitative ordering replicates across teachers. compression is Length-matched truncation: not just cheaper supervision. The Qwenteacher truncation ablation compares truncated raw traces against the two compressed sources (Appendix E). At the same training-token budget as Ministral-14B, naive truncation is substantially worse for Qwen3.5-0.8B LoRA and both Llama-3.1-8B methods. For Qwen3.5-9B and gptoss-20B, truncation is competitive with aggressive Ministral compression, but it still trails or ties the less aggressive Llama-70B compressor, and where it beats Ministral it emits longer inference traces (e.g., Qwen3.5-9B Full: 316 vs. 148 median reasoning tokens). Thus the compressed-trace result is not explained by shorter training budget alone: rewriting the trace usually preserves useful reasoning better than cutting off the raw prefix, while raw traces remain best when accuracy is the only objective. Answer-only ablation: cheap but unreliable. Dropping reasoning entirely is cheaper but consistently worse in our setup: its best configuration is Qwen3.5-9B LoRA at 0.781 overall (vs. 0.862 on raw), and LoRA at 8B/0.8B reaches 0.590/0.390 overall. Under full fine-tuning it becomes unstable: Qwen3.5-9B drops to 0.550 with 99.9% truncation, Qwen3.5-0.8B to 0.001 with 99.9% truncation, and Llama-3.1-8B to 0.181 with 2-token Figure 2: Approximate reasoning token counts: original (left), Llama-70B-compressed (centre), Ministral-14Bcompressed (right). Top row: Qwen3.5-397B teacher. Bottom row: gpt-oss-120B teacher. The gpt-oss teachers original-trace distribution is already concentrated at low token counts; compression sharpens the peak but moves it less in absolute terms than under the Qwen teacher. outputs. The median across the six answer-only runs falls well below the matching raw runs (see Appendix D).

sections.title: 6.1 Why Llama-70B Beats Ministral-14B
sections.content: Trade-off, not domination. Compressed students do not Pareto-dominate raw: raw has strictly higher accuracy, compressed has strictly fewer tokens. At 9B this trades roughly 35 accuracy points for about 510 per-token efficiency, depending on teacher and compressorworth taking when inference cost or latency dominates, not when peak accuracy does. Truncation. Raw-trace students hit the 8,192token cap on 0.615.4% of records (highest at Llama-3.1-8B and 0.8B under the Qwen teacher). Compressed-trace students truncate on 1% at the 8B/9B scales, while 20B compressed runs show small truncation tail of 0.73.6%, with gpt-oss20B Ministral-14B mild outlier at 3.23.6%; the 0.8B Full Llama-70B-compressed run under the Qwen teacher also truncates on 23.1% despite short mediansthe small full-fine-tuned student loses control of EOT, inflating mean tokens and collapsing per-token efficiency for that run. The answer-only runs are the most extreme: Qwen3.59B and Qwen3.5-0.8B saturate the cap on nearly every record. 6 traces usually beat Llama-70B-compressed Ministral-14B-compressed on accuracy, although Ministral-14B slightly leads in Qwen-teacher Qwen3.5-0.8B Full and Llama-3.1-8B LoRA. We hypothesize that difference in model capabilities could affect the student, as the dense 70B Llama3 model is stronger than the smaller Ministral model. We also find that Ministral-14B compresses more aggressively than Llama-70B (mean ρ=0.086 vs. 0.142 under the Qwen teacher, and 0.147 vs. 0.210 under the gpt-oss teacher). This has two downstream effects. First, Ministral could merge multiple reasoning steps into single steps more aggressively, discarding intermediate computation. the resulting train runs are not compute equivalent as we use the same number of epochs with different trainable token count. Ministral-14B therefore appears to be the better choice only when brevity dominates: it has the highest Acc / 1k Tokens at Llama-3.1-8B (3.93 vs. 2.87) and Qwen3.5-9B LoRA (4.86 vs. 3.71). With Qwen3.5-9B the accuracy gap between is modest, while Ministral-14B compressors can win on Acc / 1k Tokens; on gpt-oss-20B, Llama-70B leads on Acc / 1k Tokens because Ministral-14Bs 3.23.6% truncation tail inflates Second, Figure 3: Training loss across the 48-run main grid plus seven Qwen-teacher truncation ablations, one row per teacher (top: Qwen3.5-397B; bottom: gpt-oss-120B), one column per student. Bold: EMA (α=0.08); Solid: LoRA; dashed: full weight. gpt-oss-20B has no LoRA curve (Full only). Under the gpt-oss teacher, compressed sources usually finish below the raw curves, including at 0.8B, although the 0.8B Ministral runs remain close to raw. its mean completion length.

sections.title: Method Effects
sections.content: The compressed-vs-raw accuracy gap is present at every scale we evaluate, but its size depends jointly on student scale and training method. For LoRA, the gap is roughly stable across the larger students (0.862 / 0.830 / 0.815 at Qwen3.5-9B, 0.657 / 0.620 / 0.621 at Llama-3.1-8B) and narrowest at 0.528 / 0.506 / 0.500 for Qwen3.5-0.8B (raw / Llama-70B / Ministral-14B). For full finetuning, the gap is comparable at 9B (0.866 / 0.834 / 0.817), 8B (0.715 / 0.665 / 0.652), and 20B (0.815 / 0.769 / 0.754); at 0.8B the Full row is 0.532 / 0.481 / 0.500, with the Llama-70B-compressed run partly affected by the truncation discussed below. Two effects plausibly combine. Smaller students have limited capacity to imitate long deliberative reasoning, so the marginal benefit of the teachers full exploration shrinks at 0.8B, narrowing the LoRA gap. And full fine-tuning at 0.8B aggressively fits the compressed format on most examples while losing control of the EOT distribution on tail of harder questions (the 23% truncation in the 0.8B Llama-70B Full run); LoRAs lower-capacity adaptation does not exhibit this pattern. Compressed traces are therefore most cost-effective for LoRA at every scale, but for full fine-tuning the case is weakest at the smallest scale we test. The answer-only baseline amplifies this interaction: at Qwen3.5-9B LoRA it is genuinely competitive (0.781 vs. 0.862 raw), but in every other configuration it collapses, indicating that the instabilities that hurt the 0.8B compressed runs also dominate the answer-only setting once the response distribution loses its reasoning structure.

sections.title: 6.3 Compression Transfers Brevity
sections.content: A robust effect across all four students and both teachers is that compression transfers brevity to inference: student trained on traces with median length produces outputs of similar median length. Under the Qwen teacher, median student reasoning is 1,0041,085 chars (Llama-70Bcompressed) and 685745 chars (Ministral-14Bcompressed) regardless of student scale; under the gpt-oss teacher these medians sit at 8311,020 / 612717 chars (Llama-70B / Ministral-14B), reflecting shorter compressed outputs in absolute terms despite milder compression ratios. Combined with 1% truncation at the 8B/9B scales and most 20B runs, this is the property that drives per-token efficiency. The truncated ablation shows that token-budget matching does not automatically transfer the same brevity. Even when truncated traces match Ministrals training length by construction, the result7 Figure 4: Per-student accuracy vs. median reasoning token count (log scale, IQR error bars) for the reasoning-trace runs and Qwen-teacher truncation ablations. Compression moves every student leftward (shorter); at 8B/9B/20B it also moves them slightly downward. The truncated ablation isolates token-budget reduction from model-based trace rewriting. ing students often decode longer than Ministraltrained students (Qwen3.5-9B Full: 316 vs. 148 median reasoning tokens; gpt-oss-20B Full: 249 vs. 156). The learned output policy therefore depends on the structure of the shortened trace, not only its token count.

sections.title: 6.4 Domain-Specific Accuracy Gaps
sections.content: The aggregate raw-vs-compressed gap hides consistent per-domain structure (Appendix D, Table 7). Across the three larger students under the Qwen teacher Full, Medicine shows the largest raw advantage (4.78.5 points) and Science the smallest (1.44.0 points): medical traces carry dense factual scaffolding (drug names, dosages, contraindications) with low intrinsic redundancy, so each dropped token costs more than token from verbose derivation. Math is the exceptionat Llama-3.1-8B under the Qwen teacher, compression beats raw (Full: 0.671 vs. 0.702 / 0.681; LoRA: 0.458 vs. 0.581 / 0.546). The mechanism is truncation: Llama3.1-8B raw under the Qwen teacher truncates on 6.115.4% of records (highest of any student) and these truncations concentrate on long symbolic traces, while compressed students truncate on 1% and reach the answer. Where the raw student is comfortably below the context cap (Qwen3.59B, gpt-oss-20B), Math reverts to modest raw advantage of few points. The practical implication: prefer raw for medical-heavy workloads and any setting where the student would not otherwise truncate; compressions accuracy cost is negligible (or negative) elsewhere.

sections.title: 7 Conclusion
sections.content: We studied post-hoc model-based compression of reasoning traces. Across 48-run main grid plus seven Qwen-teacher truncation ablations, compression cuts training tokens to 1230% of raw, wall-clock training by 2.07.6, and median inference reasoning by roughly 319. The cost is accuracy: raw teacher traces remain best at every scale and teacher, although the gap is smallest at 0.8B LoRA. The answer-only baseline is competitive only at Qwen3.5-9B LoRA and collapses under full fine-tuning; at 0.8B Full, one Llama-compressed run also fails to terminate on 23.1% of records. The truncation ablation shows compression is not merely token-count artifact: naive raw-prefix truncation often loses to modelcompressed traces at the same or lower inference cost, and never recovers raw-trace accuracy. The findings replicate across teachers, while the win tracks teacher verbosity. Future work should explore answer-preservation verification, domainspecific compression prompts, and wider sweep of teachers.

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: Single, generic compression prompt. We use one compression prompt across all domains. domain-specific prompt (for example, preserve all symbolic computation verbatim for mathematics) could change the per-domain accuracy gap and is not explored here. Truncation. We use an 8,192-token decoding cap. Across both teachers, raw-trace students truncate on 0.61.8% (Qwen3.5-9B), 1.815.4% (Llama-3.1-8B), 2.58.6% (Qwen3.5-0.8B), and 0.73.2% (gpt-oss-20B) of records, so the reported reasoning-length advantage of compression is, at the larger scales, slight underestimate. The 0.8B Llama-70B Full compressed run under the Qwen teacher additionally truncates on 23.1% of records despite its short median outputs, which suppresses its per-token efficiency relative to the LoRA counterpart; we discuss this anomaly in Section 5.4. The answer-only runs are the most extreme: Qwen3.5-9B and Qwen3.5-0.8B saturate the cap on essentially every record. These failure modes indicate that the base model may require different training setup to learn zero-shot answering.

sections.title: G Faithfulness of Compressed Traces
sections.content: Section 3 treats the compressor MC as black box: it takes (qi, ti, ai) and returns shorter ˆti that is assumed to preserve the answer-bearing reasoning. Because the canonical answer ai is verified against before compression and re-appended unchanged after, compressor that silently corrupts the reasoning merging two derivation steps that should have remained separate, hallucinating an intermediate constant, dropping case in case splitwould still produce syntactically well-formed training example, and the student would learn to imitate the corruption. The downstream-accuracy gap between raw and compressed runs is an indirect upper bound on the cost of such corruptions (Section 7), but it conflates the trace lost load-bearing step with the student failed to imitate the compressed format. This appendix describes the separate, trace-level check we run to disentangle the two and reports what it finds. G.1 Method For each compressed trace ˆti produced in Stage 2, we re-issue the triple (qi, ti, ˆti, judge. The judge is asked to compare ˆti against ti along three axes and to emit structured verdict: Faithfulness (15): is every claim, calculation, and conclusion in ˆti supported by or consistent with ti? faithful compression may omit redundant exploration, false starts, and verbosity, but it must not introduce unsupported claims. ) to third LLM, the Coverage (15): are the essential reasoning steps needed to support preserved in ˆti? Clarity (15): is the compressed trace coherent and usable as deliberation? Verdict: one of faithful, partially_faithful, unfaithful. Answer alignment: Boolean indicating whether ˆti, read on its own, would support the canonical answer . Failure modes: (possibly empty) subset of {contradiction, unsupported_claim, omitted_key_step, answer_mismatch, overcompression, incoherent, empty_original, empty_compressed}. Rationale: short free-text justification. The verdict is constrained server-side by JSON schema (response_format with Pydantic-derived spec), so structural validation is essentially free; the only realistic parse failure is response truncated at the judges max_completion_tokens. The full prompt includes the question, the canonical answer, both traces (middle-truncated to 40k and 12k characters respectively if longer, so the judge always sees both ends), and an explicit instruction that omissions of redundant exploration are acceptable but unsupported claims are not. We use gpt-oss-120B as the judge using structured decoding to ensure predictable output that can be easily parsed. G.2 Results Table 10 reports the judges verdict distribution per (compressor, domain, dataset). For each compressor we report the number of traces assessed (N ), mean faithfulness score (15), the percentage rated faithful (verdict, not score), and the percentage with positive answer_alignment. Headline numbers. The judge rates the overwhelming majority of compressed traces as faithful (verdict-level, not score). Across the full 283,260-trace corpus, the Llama-70B compressor scores 98.1% faithful with mean faithfulness 4.98/5 and 97.4% positive answer alignment; the Ministral-14B compressor scores 98.0% faithful, 4.97/5, and 97.2% alignment. Verdict-level rates are above 95% in every (compressor, dataset) configuration except GPQA Diamond (9093%), where the small sample and the difficulty of the source material together explain the dip. Failure-mode breakdown. The remaining 2% of non-faithful verdicts are dominated by omitted_key_step, answer_mismatch, and unsupported_claim; overcompression, contradiction, and incoherent are rare. empty_original / empty_compressed are essentially zero, confirming that Stage 2 rarely degenerates to degenerate output.

sections.content: 87,441 7,186 416 960 1,079 135 8,593 395 167,277 9,778 283,260 4.97 5.00 4.97 4. 4.99 4.76 4.98 4.98 4.98 4.98 4.98 98.3 99.7 98.8 99.6 95.8 90. 96.0 98.2 97.9 98.7 98.1 97.3 85.8 88.7 92.3 97.6 92.6 98. 99.0 97.9 99.1 87,441 7,186 416 960 1,079 135 8,593 395 167,277 9,778 97. 283,260 4.95 4.99 4.99 4.99 4.97 4.75 4.97 4.96 4.97 4.97 4. 97.8 99.6 99.8 99.4 97.0 92.6 97.9 98.2 98.0 98.3 98.0 96.5 85.4 90.9 94. 97.0 94.8 98.4 98.7 97.9 99.3 97.2 Table 10: LLM-as-judge faithfulness assessment of compressed traces, scored by gpt-oss-120B. Faith. is the mean faithfulness score (15). % Faithful is the percentage assigned verdict faithful (the remainder is split between partially_faithful and unfaithful). % Align is the percentage with positive answer alignment: the judge, given , agrees that the compressed trace supports it. Interpretation. The 2% non-faithful rate is small relative to the raw-vs-compressed accuracy gap reported in Section 5.4 ( 8.5 points at 8B), so the judge data is consistent with the limitationssection claim that propagated trace corruption is small but non-zero contributor and not the dominant explanation for the gap. The remaining gap is then accounted for by the legitimate cost of stripping redundant deliberation that the student would otherwise have imitated.

sections.title: H Training Stack and Compute
sections.content: This appendix records the implementation and compute used to produce the 48 main-grid student runs, seven Qwen-teacher truncation ablations, and the upstream trace-generation and compression stages. H.1 Hardware All runs use NVIDIA H100 GPUs (SXM, 80 GB) connected by NVLink/NVSwitch within node and InfiniBand between nodes. Each student training run uses single node; the per-job GPU count depends on the student and method (4 GPUs for the 0.8B student and for the 8B/9B LoRA-on-compressed runs that fit on 4 GPUs, 8 GPUs for the 8B/9B raw and full-FSDP runs and for every gpt-oss-20B run). The exact GPU count per run is recorded in Table 11. Trace generation and trace compression use 8 H100s on single node. H.2 Trace Generation and Compression Stack Both upstream stages use vLLM with continuous batching and FP8 weights for the teacher. Teacher (Stage 1). Qwen3.5-397B-A17B-FP8 served on 8H100 with tensor parallelism 8, data parallelism 1, max model length 16,384, GPU memory utilisation 0.92, and the qwen3 reasoning parser. Sampling batch size is 512; we use rejection sampling with single attempt per remaining question per round. Compressor: Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct. Served on 8H100 with tensor parallelism 4 and data parallelism 2 (two replicas, each 4 GPUs). Max model length 16,384, GPU memory utilisation 0.92, temperature 0.3, batch size 1,024. Compressor: Ministral-3-14B-Instruct-2512. Served on 8H100 with tensor parallelism 1 and data parallelism 8 (one replica per GPU). Max model length 16,384, GPU memory utilisation 0.92, temperature 0.3, batch size 4,096. 19 H.3 Student Training Stack All student runs use Axolotl with the following common configuration: chat template inherited from the tokenizer, EOT token <im_end>, train_on_eot: true, BF16 mixed precision with TF32 enabled, sequence length 16,384, sample packing with padding to sequence length, micro batch size 1, gradient accumulation 8, cosine schedule with 10% warmup, FlashAttention 2, the CutCrossEntropy plugin, and the Liger plugin (fused RMSNorm, RoPE, GLU activation, and gated RMSNorm). turn, split_thinking: LoRA. Rank 64, α=32, dropout 0.05, applied to {q,k,v,o,gate,up,down}_proj. Learning rate 1104, one epoch, optimizer adamw_bnb_8bit, gradient checkpointing on. Full fine-tuning (0.8B). Learning rate 2105, one epoch, optimizer adamw_torch_8bit. (8B / (FULL_SHARD), Full fine-tuning TRANSFORMER_BASED_WRAP auto-wrap (Qwen3_5DecoderLayer, LlamaDecoderLayer, or GptOssDecoderLayer), reshard after forward, activation checkpointing, full state dict. Same learning rate and epoch count as the 0.8B full setting. 20B). FSDP v2 with sharding 9B / full Answer-only ablation. The answer-only data source is produced by stripping the entire <think>. . . </think> block from each correct trace, leaving only the user question and the final answer. Training uses the same chat template, optimiser, schedule, and epoch count as the matching reasoning-trace configuration; the only difference is the training data. H.4 Compute per Run Table 11 reports wall-clock time and GPU-hours per student run. Wall-clock numbers are taken from Table 6; GPU-hours are wall-clock per-job GPU count (4 or 8, depending on the run). Token counts are the source-dataset trainable-token totals (raw, compressed, truncated, or answer-only, before the epoch multiplier), matching the right half of Table 6. Aggregate compute. Summed across the 55 student runs, training consumes 280.9 H100-hours of pure GPU time. The seven truncation ablations add 16.0 H100-h over the 264.9 H100-h main grid. Within each student, the raw-trace and full-FSDP runs dominate (e.g., Qwen3.5-9B raw under the Qwen teacher alone accounts for 26.7 H100-h), and the answer-only, truncated, and compressed runs together account for less than third of the per-student total. Trace generation and compression still dominate the project budget: the teacher pass over 345,383 questions and the two compressor passes over the 283,335 / 281,911 correct traces from each teacher each occupy an 8H100 node. Upstream wall-clock. On the same 8H100 node, the teacher pass over 345,383 questions took 43.5 ( 348 H100-h); compressing the 283,335 correct traces took 1 09 with Ministral-3-14B ( 9.2 H100-h) and 8 19 with Llama-3.3-70B ( 66.5 H100-h), for 75.7 H100-h of compression in total. The upstream stages thus contribute 424 H100-hours, roughly 1.5 the student-training total of 280.9 H100-hours. H.5 Energy and Carbon Footprint Student training was performed on H100 GPUs hosted in Finnish datacentre. We estimate energy and carbon at the GPU-hour level using the per-GPU SXM TDP (700 W) multiplied by an assumed datacentre PUE of 1.15, giving an effective draw of 805 per H100-hour including cooling and infrastructure overhead. Estimated emissions. Student training plus upstream trace generation and compression amount to 704.9 0.805 567.4 kWh. We use 79 gCO2eq/kWh, the published Finnish annual average for 2023 (Fingrid / IEA) where the servers are hosted; 2024 was slightly lower. We estimate total of 567.4 0.079 44.8 kgCO2eq.
